stem: In y = 3x + 5, what does the coefficient 3 (the slope) mean?

options.text: y starts at 3
For every 1 unit increase in x, y increases by 3
The line crosses the y-axis at 3
x is always 3 times larger than y

explanation_after: The slope (coefficient of x) is the rate of change. A slope of 3 means: for every 1 unit increase in x, y increases by 3 units. The intercept (5) is where the line crosses the y-axis (x=0).

stem: R² = 0.85 for a linear regression model means:

options.text: The model is correct 85% of the time
85% of the variance in y is explained by the model's features
The prediction error is 15%
15% of predictions are wrong

explanation_after: R² measures the proportion of variance in the target explained by the model. R²=0.85 means 85% of variation is captured; 15% is unexplained noise or missing features.

stem: What does a negative coefficient in linear regression mean?

options.text: The feature is unimportant
As the feature increases, the prediction decreases (negative relationship)
The model made an error
The feature should be removed

explanation_after: A negative coefficient means as that feature increases, the predicted value decreases. For example, 'distance from city centre' having coefficient -3000 means houses farther from the centre are predicted to cost £3000 less per km.

stem: What is the purpose of gradient descent in linear regression?

options.text: To find the exact closed-form solution
To iteratively adjust weights to minimise the prediction error, useful when the exact solution is too expensive
To visualise the data
To select features

explanation_after: The closed-form OLS solution requires inverting an n×n matrix — O(n³). For large datasets, gradient descent iteratively improves weights by taking small steps in the direction of decreasing loss, running in O(nd) per step.

stem: Logistic regression differs from linear regression because:

options.text: It uses a different programming library
It outputs a probability between 0 and 1 via a sigmoid function, used for classification
It can only handle two features
It does not use weights

explanation_after: Logistic regression applies a sigmoid (logistic) function to the linear output: P(y=1) = σ(wx+b). This squishes any value into [0,1], making it a probability estimate for binary classification.

stem: What is multicollinearity and why is it a problem for linear regression?

options.text: When the target variable has multiple modes
When two or more features are highly correlated, making individual coefficients unstable and hard to interpret
When the model has too many layers
When R² is above 0.99

explanation_after: When features are correlated (e.g. 'size_sqft' and 'size_m2'), the model can't determine which one explains the outcome. Coefficients become unstable — small data changes cause wild swings. Variance Inflation Factor (VIF) detects this.
